funding_request_details.how_will_the_fun.answer:
We are requesting RoundUP Program funds to support the launch and first full growing season of the Stoughton Community Youth Garden Project, located on a vacant lot adjacent to the Stoughton Public Library’s south parking area.

Funds will be used for the following specific purposes:

1. Garden Infrastructure and Supplies ($1,200)
   - Purchase of 10 cedar raised garden beds (4' x 8') to create accessible growing spaces for youth, including two beds that are elevated for participants with mobility challenges.
   - Soil and compost delivery (approximately 8 cubic yards of organic garden soil and 4 cubic yards of compost) from a local landscape supplier in Dane County.
   - Drip irrigation components (hoses, connectors, timers, and a simple rain barrel system) to conserve water and allow youth to learn about sustainable watering practices.
   - Basic tools sized for youth: 8 hand trowels, 8 cultivators, 6 child-sized shovels, 6 rakes, 6 watering cans, and 2 wheelbarrows. We will label and store these in a small lockable outdoor storage box.

2. Seeds, Plants, and Educational Materials ($500)
   - Seeds and starter plants focused on culturally relevant and kid-friendly produce (e.g., tomatoes, peppers, green beans, carrots, cucumbers, kale, herbs, and a small pollinator flower section). We plan to purchase from local nurseries in Stoughton and Madison.
   - Printed educational materials for our 12-week curriculum, including laminated plant ID cards, simple garden journals for each participating youth, and weather-resistant signage in English and Spanish describing each bed and the garden rules.
   - A small library of 10–15 kid-friendly gardening and nutrition books that will be shared between the garden site and the Stoughton Public Library children’s section.

3. Program Costs and Safety Items ($300)
   - Healthy snacks for youth participants during weekly garden sessions (e.g., fresh fruit, granola bars, and water) with attention to common allergies.
   - First-aid kit and basic safety equipment, including youth-sized gardening gloves, sunscreen, and insect repellent.
   - Printing costs for parent/guardian consent forms, bilingual flyers, and end-of-season celebration invitations.

4. Community Harvest and Celebration ($250)
   - Supplies for a community "Garden Harvest Day" at the end of the season, including reusable serving trays, compostable plates and utensils, and simple ingredients to prepare a few garden-based dishes (such as salsa, cucumber salad, and herb-infused water).
   - Materials for a small recognition display at the Stoughton Public Library, highlighting youth artwork, garden photos, and a thank-you poster acknowledging the RoundUP Program’s support.

All funds will be tracked in a simple line-item budget managed by our volunteer treasurer. We will prioritize purchasing from local businesses in Stoughton whenever possible (e.g., Stoughton Garden Center, local print shops, and grocery stores) to keep the economic benefit within the community.

funding_request_details.what_are_the_ben.answer:
The Stoughton Community Youth Garden Project is designed to provide layered benefits that touch youth, families, schools, and the broader community. The RoundUP Program funds will help us turn an underused piece of land into an active, educational, and welcoming space.

1. Youth Development and Education
   - Hands-on learning: Many children in Stoughton, especially those living in apartments or homes without yards, have limited opportunities to see where food comes from. Through weekly garden sessions, 3rd–8th grade students will learn basic plant biology, soil health, composting, and seasonal cycles by actually planting, tending, and harvesting crops.
   - Life skills and responsibility: Youth will be assigned rotating roles (watering lead, tool manager, harvest recorder, etc.), teaching responsibility, time management, and teamwork. We will have them sign a simple “garden agreement” so they can practice commitment and follow-through.
   - Nutrition and healthy habits: Our curriculum includes tasting sessions and simple cooking demonstrations using garden produce. For example, one of our volunteers (a retired home economics teacher from Stoughton High School) will show students how to prepare quick, affordable snacks like carrot sticks with herb yogurt dip or cucumber and tomato salad. This exposure is especially valuable for kids who may be hesitant to try vegetables.

2. Community Building and Inclusion
   - Bringing neighbors together: The garden is centrally located near the library and within walking distance of several apartment complexes. Parents, grandparents, and neighbors will be invited to drop in during garden hours, ask questions, and join harvest days. We hope this becomes a place where people who might not normally cross paths can meet and talk.
   - Intergenerational connections: We have already recruited three senior volunteers from the Stoughton Senior Center who grew up on farms and are excited to help teach. They will share stories about how food was grown and preserved in earlier decades, giving youth a sense of local history and continuity.
   - Cultural sharing: Stoughton has a mix of families with Norwegian, Hmong, Mexican, and other backgrounds. We plan to invite families to suggest crops that are meaningful to them (for example, cilantro and hot peppers for salsa, specific beans or herbs used in traditional dishes). This gives youth a way to see their culture represented and valued.

3. Food Access and Support for Local Families
   - Fresh produce for families: A portion of the harvest will go home with participating youth and their families, which can help stretch grocery budgets during the summer months. We will create simple recipe cards so families know how to use the produce.
   - Donations to local food programs: Any surplus will be donated to the Stoughton Food Pantry and to the free community meal program hosted by a local church. We have already spoken with a pantry volunteer who is enthusiastic about offering more fresh, locally grown produce.

4. Beautification and Environmental Benefits
   - Transforming an underused lot: The current lot is mostly bare soil and patchy grass. By installing raised beds, flowers, and a small pollinator section, the space will become more attractive and inviting for people walking to the library or nearby businesses.
   - Pollinator support and environmental education: Our pollinator bed will include native flowers like coneflower and black-eyed susan. Youth will learn why pollinators are important, how to support them, and how thoughtful planting can make a difference.
   - Sustainable practices: The use of drip irrigation, rain barrels, composting, and mulch will demonstrate practical ways to conserve water and reduce waste. Youth will learn that these practices are doable at home, even on a small scale.

5. Partnership with Existing Community Institutions
   - Collaboration with the Stoughton Public Library: The library has agreed to co-host some of our educational sessions, such as a “Garden Story Time” for younger siblings and a small display of gardening books. This partnership strengthens the library’s role as a community hub and may draw in families who don’t regularly use library services.
   - Support for local schools: While this is a community-based project, we have spoken with teachers at Kegonsa and Fox Prairie Elementary Schools who are interested in tying the garden to science and health standards. We will share photos, simple lesson ideas, and invite classes for scheduled visits.

Overall, the project will help Stoughton become a place where children grow up with a stronger connection to their food, their environment, and their neighbors. The RoundUP funds will make it possible for us to remove financial barriers so that any interested child—regardless of family income—can participate fully without paying fees or purchasing their own supplies.

funding_request_details.what_other_infor.answer:
Background and Origin of the Project:
The idea for the Stoughton Community Youth Garden Project grew out of conversations at the Stoughton Public Library’s parent discussion group in late 2023. Several of us—parents, library staff, and a few local retirees—noticed that our kids were spending a lot of time indoors, especially after the pandemic, and that many families were struggling with rising food prices. I (Rachel Lindstrom) grew up in a small Wisconsin town where my grandmother maintained a large vegetable garden, and I saw firsthand how gardening can build confidence, patience, and a sense of accomplishment in kids.

Over the past six months, a small volunteer committee of seven community members has been meeting twice a month to plan this project. We have:
- Secured verbal permission from the City of Stoughton to use the vacant lot adjacent to the library’s south parking area, pending final insurance documentation.
- Connected with a UW–Extension Dane County master gardener who has offered to advise on soil testing, crop rotation, and safe gardening practices for youth.
- Spoken with staff at the Stoughton Senior Center, who have helped recruit older adults interested in volunteering as “garden grandparents.”

Participants and Program Structure:
We plan to start with 15–20 youth participants in our first season to ensure we can provide close supervision and meaningful hands-on experience. Sessions will run once a week for 90 minutes from late May through early September. A typical session will look like this:
- 10–15 minutes: Welcome circle, check-in, and review of the day’s plan.
- 45–60 minutes: Hands-on gardening tasks (planting, weeding, watering, harvesting), with youth divided into small teams.
- 15–20 minutes: Short educational activity (for example, comparing different types of soil, observing insects, or measuring plant growth).
- 10–15 minutes: Snack time and reflection, where kids share what they learned or found surprising.

We will prioritize enrollment for youth who:
- Live within walking or biking distance of the garden.
- May not have access to outdoor growing space at home.
- Are recommended by school counselors or teachers as benefiting from positive, structured after-school activities.

Risk Management and Safety:
We take safety seriously, especially since we’ll be working with minors. Our safety plan includes:
- All regular adult volunteers (those present more than three sessions) will complete background checks through a local service that the library already uses for some of its programs.
- We will maintain a minimum adult-to-youth ratio of 1:5.
- Parents/guardians will sign consent and emergency contact forms. We will ask about allergies, medical conditions, and any physical limitations so we can adapt activities.
- Youth will be required to wear closed-toe shoes, and we will provide gloves and encourage the use of sunscreen and insect repellent.

Sustainability and Long-Term Vision:
Our intent is not to run a one-time program, but to establish a garden that can grow and evolve with the community over several years. To support this:
- We are creating a simple volunteer handbook and step-by-step seasonal checklist so new volunteers can easily join and understand the routines.
- We are documenting our curriculum (lesson plans, supply lists, timelines) so that in future years, other community members—or even local teachers—could replicate or adapt what we’ve done.
- We plan to seek additional small grants or sponsorships from local businesses (for example, hardware stores, garden centers, and grocery stores in Stoughton) to cover ongoing costs like seeds, soil amendments, and replacement tools.

Personal Note:
As the parent of two children at Fox Prairie Elementary (ages 9 and 11), I’ve seen how much calmer and more confident they become after spending time outdoors doing something purposeful. When we piloted a very small version of this idea last summer in my own backyard with four neighborhood kids, one of the participants—a 10-year-old who is usually very quiet—asked at the end of the season if he could take home a single tomato plant to “keep it going.” His mother later told me that he checked on that plant every day and started asking more questions about cooking and nutrition. That small experience convinced me that a larger, community-based garden could have a meaningful impact on many more children in Stoughton.

We are deeply appreciative of the consideration from the RoundUP Program and are happy to provide periodic updates, photos, or a brief written summary at the end of the season to show how the funds were used and what we learned in the process.
